annotation_target.structure: Opening antecedent of the Adagio cantabile (paired continuation at m. 5–8). The right hand is exceptionally sparse — only two pitches sound across the four bars (C4 at m. 1 b3.9979 and D#4 at m. 3 b3.9354, both at the very end of their measures as upbeat-pickup leads into the next bar). The harmonic and rhythmic engine lives in the left hand: 16 LH events forming a slow rocking arpeggio that outlines the A♭ major tonic and its V (E♭, MIDI-spelled D#) across mm. 2–4. The LH pattern alternates A♭ (G#2, G#3, MIDI naming) with E♭ (D#3) and brief chromatic-color tones (A#3, C#3, G3, C3). The dominant pitch class throughout is D# (E♭) — it is the V of A♭ major and recurs 7 times across the phrase, anchoring the harmonic field.
key_moments: m. 1 b3.9979 — C4 entrance in the right hand (MIDI velocity 63, sustained 2.35 seconds across the bar line into m. 2). This is the only right-hand event in measure 1; the entire bar otherwise rests. The C4 pickup leads into the LH arpeggio that begins at m. 2 b0.0188 with G#2 (A♭2) on the bass downbeat

m. 2 — LH rocking arpeggio entry: G#2 (A♭2) bass at b0.0188, G#3 (A♭3) inner voice at b0.1271, then D#3 (E♭3) at b1.2979 marks the first dominant-tone arrival, G#3 again at b2.2979, D#3 again at b3.1896; the surface is six LH attacks across the bar (the densest LH measure of the phrase)

m. 3 — chromatic-color cluster: LH attacks A#3 (B♭3, the seventh) at b0.0771, C#3 (D♭3, lowered ♮IV color) at b0.1438, G3 (the leading tone of A♭) at b0.2146, then D#3 at b1.0438, G3 at b2.0229, D#3 at b2.9771. The right hand re-enters with D#4 (E♭4) at b3.9354 — the second and final RH pickup, sustained 3.01 seconds (longest RH duration of the phrase)

m. 4 — closing LH arpeggio: G#3 at b0.0229, C3 (low tonic) at b0.0604 (sustained 2.04 seconds — the longest single LH bass note), D#3 at b1.0729, G#3 at b2.0271, D#3 at b3.0854. The phrase ends with the LH still rocking, the RH silent — the opening sound is suspension, not arrival

hand balance: the left hand plays 16 notes across the phrase; the right hand plays 2. The LH dominates this opening texture by a wide margin — the cantabile melody is implied by the C4 / D#4 pickup pair, not stated in full
teaching_goals: treat the LH rocking arpeggio as the main musical content of this phrase — it carries the harmony, the rhythm, and the registral motion while the RH is sparse
voice each RH pickup (C4 at m. 1 b3.9979 and D#4 at m. 3 b3.9354) as a singing upbeat that bridges into the next bar — sustain its tone through the LH activity below
feel the recurring D#3 (E♭3) attacks as the dominant pole of A♭ major; the bar-by-bar rocking returns to E♭ as the gravitational center
release the LH C3 in m. 4 b0.0604 only when its 2.04-second sustain naturally ends — it is the harmonic floor for the entire closing bar
style_tips: pp to mp throughout — extremely soft, contemplative; never loud enough to break the dreamlike opening
pedal each LH chord change (m. 2, m. 3, m. 4) — half-pedal through the inner-voice activity to keep the rocking arpeggio clear without smearing the chromatic-color tones
Adagio means very slow — the RH C4 should ring 2+ seconds before the LH bass arrives at m. 2; do not rush the pickup

teaching_notes.note: The right-hand C4 at b3.9979 (display: beat 4 of m. 1) is the phrase's signature opening pickup. It enters alone — no LH event sounds in m. 1 — and sustains 2.35 seconds across the bar line into m. 2. Voice it as the cantabile entry; the entire phrase grows out of this single soft tone.
technique: voice the C4 entrance as a singing pickup

teaching_notes.note: Six LH attacks: G#2 (A♭2) at b0.0188, G#3 (A♭3) at b0.1271, D#3 (E♭3) at b1.2979, G#3 at b2.2979, D#3 at b3.1896. The pattern outlines A♭ major's I–V rocking — A♭ bass with G3-G#3 inner-voice motion and D#3 (E♭3) as the V tone. The surface rhythm is even and quiet; the inner motion is the musical content.
technique: let the D#3 (E♭3) speak as the dominant tone

teaching_notes.note: Chromatic-color cluster at the start of the bar: A#3 (B♭3) at b0.0771, C#3 (D♭3) at b0.1438, G3 at b0.2146 — three attacks within the first quarter-beat. The right hand re-enters with D#4 (E♭4) at b3.9354, the longest RH pitch of the phrase (3.01 seconds). Voice D#4 as the answering pickup to C4 — together they sketch the cantabile melody.
technique: voice the chromatic LH cluster lightly
feel the C4/D#4 pair as the implied melody

teaching_notes.note: The LH C3 at b0.0604 sustains 2.04 seconds — the lowest and longest LH bass note of the phrase. Above it, the rocking arpeggio continues with G#3, D#3, G#3, D#3. The phrase ends with the LH still active, the RH silent. There is no resolution — it is an open suspension that the continuation (m. 5–8) will answer.

target_trace.objective: Read mm. 1–4 of Pathetique Sonata, 2nd Movement (Adagio cantabile), view the piano roll, analyze the phrase, play in a loop, and predict the continuation.

session.content: Show me measures 1–4 of Beethoven's Pathetique Sonata second movement and explain the singing theme.
Let me view the piano roll for mm. 1–4.

The Adagio cantabile opens with one of Beethoven's most beautiful melodies in Ab major. Measures 1–4 present the antecedent: a long, singing RH melody over a rocking LH arpeggio pattern. The mood is peaceful and contemplative. The phrase ends on the dominant Eb — expectant. Let me loop mm. 1–4.
Pathetique mvt 2 mm. 1–4: Adagio cantabile opening, Ab major singing melody over LH arpeggios. Ends on dominant Eb. Teaching focus: sustained singing tone, LH rocking pattern.
